title: Mutualism in Ecosystems
passage: In many ecosystems, organisms form partnerships that benefit both participants, a relationship biologists call mutualism. Certain flowering plants, for instance, produce sugary nectar to ____(1)____ insects, which in turn carry pollen from bloom to bloom. Because neither species could reproduce as efficiently alone, the association confers a clear ____(2)____ on both. Over long spans of time, such interactions can become so tightly linked that the two species evolve in tandem, each exerting selective pressure on the other. Scientists regard these coevolved systems as compelling ____(3)____ that cooperation, no less than competition, has shaped the diversity of life on Earth.

questions.q: Blank (1): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Plants offer nectar to draw insects in, so 'attract' fits. 'Repel' is the opposite; 'detach' and 'confine' do not collocate with luring pollinators.

questions.q: Blank (2): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: A mutual benefit is an 'advantage.' 'Obligation,' 'restriction,' and 'hazard' all imply a cost or burden, contradicting the mutual gain described.

questions.q: Blank (3): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Coevolved systems serve as 'evidence' for a scientific claim. 'Speculation' and 'coincidence' undercut the certainty implied by 'compelling'; 'exception' reverses the point.

title: The Birth of Stars
passage: Stars are born within vast clouds of gas and dust known as nebulae. When a region of such a cloud grows dense enough, gravity causes it to ____(1)____, drawing material inward toward a central core. As the core contracts, its temperature and pressure rise until hydrogen nuclei begin to fuse, releasing enormous energy. This outward flow of radiation eventually ____(2)____ the inward pull of gravity, and the young star settles into a stable state. Astronomers cannot watch a single star form from start to finish, for the process ____(3)____ millions of years, far longer than any human lifetime or recorded history.

questions.q: Blank (1): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Gravity pulling matter inward describes a gravitational 'collapse.' 'Expand' and 'disperse' mean the opposite; 'rotate' does not capture the inward motion.

questions.q: Blank (2): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Radiation pressure offsetting gravity so the star stabilizes means it 'balances' gravity. 'Accelerates' and 'magnifies' would intensify collapse; 'conceals' is meaningless here.

questions.q: Blank (3): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: A process lasting millions of years 'spans' that time. 'Repeats' and 'precedes' misstate the relationship, and 'halts' contradicts an ongoing process.

title: Linear Perspective in Renaissance Art
passage: During the Italian Renaissance, painters developed a technique called linear perspective, which allowed them to represent three-dimensional space on a flat surface with unprecedented realism. By making parallel lines appear to ____(1)____ at a single vanishing point, artists could create a convincing illusion of depth. This innovation did more than improve visual accuracy; it reflected a broader cultural shift toward observation and mathematical order. Wealthy patrons came to ____(2)____ works that demonstrated such technical mastery, and the new style spread rapidly across Europe. Today art historians ____(3)____ perspective as one of the defining achievements of the period.

questions.q: Blank (1): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Parallel lines meeting at a vanishing point 'converge.' 'Diverge' is the opposite direction; 'fluctuate' and 'dissolve' do not describe lines meeting at a point.

questions.q: Blank (2): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Patrons valuing skilled works came to 'prize' them. 'Neglect' and 'distrust' imply the opposite reaction; 'conceal' does not fit the sense of appreciation.

questions.q: Blank (3): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Historians 'regard' perspective as a major achievement (regard X as Y). 'Dismiss' and 'overlook' contradict its celebrated status; 'postpone' does not fit.

title: Plate Tectonics
passage: The theory of plate tectonics holds that Earth's rigid outer shell is broken into several large plates that slowly move over a hotter, softer layer beneath them. Where two plates collide, one may be forced downward beneath the other, a process that can ____(1)____ powerful earthquakes. Elsewhere, plates pull apart, allowing molten rock to rise and ____(2)____ new crust along the seafloor. Although these movements amount to only a few centimeters each year, their ____(3)____ effects over millions of years are immense, reshaping coastlines and raising entire mountain ranges high above the surrounding land.

questions.q: Blank (1): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Subduction can 'trigger' earthquakes (set them off). 'Prevent' is the opposite; 'absorb' and 'postpone' do not collocate with causing earthquakes.

questions.q: Blank (2): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Rising molten rock creates crust, so it 'forms' new crust. 'Erode,' 'dissolve,' and 'consume' all describe destruction rather than creation.

questions.q: Blank (3): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Tiny yearly motions adding up over ages are 'cumulative.' 'Immediate' and 'temporary' clash with 'over millions of years'; 'negligible' contradicts 'immense.'

title: Understanding Inflation
passage: Economists use the term inflation to describe a sustained rise in the general level of prices across an economy. When prices climb, each unit of currency buys fewer goods, so the purchasing power of money ____(1)____. Moderate inflation is generally considered harmless, and central banks often aim for a small, predictable rate. Rapid or unpredictable inflation, however, can ____(2)____ economic stability, discouraging saving and complicating long-term planning. To keep prices in check, policymakers may raise interest rates, a measure intended to ____(3)____ the consumer demand that pushes prices steadily upward across markets.

questions.q: Blank (1): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: If money buys fewer goods, its purchasing power 'declines.' 'Soars' is the opposite; 'stabilizes' and 'fluctuates' ignore the clear downward direction stated.

questions.q: Blank (2): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Harmful inflation 'undermines' stability. 'Reinforce,' 'guarantee,' and 'restore' all suggest strengthening stability, the opposite of the harm described.

questions.q: Blank (3): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Raising rates aims to 'curb' (restrain) demand. 'Stimulate' and 'accelerate' would increase demand; 'satisfy' does not fit a policy meant to reduce it.

title: How Memories Are Formed
passage: Not everything we experience becomes a lasting memory. Psychologists distinguish between short-term memory, which holds information only briefly, and long-term memory, which can ____(1)____ information for years. The transfer between the two depends on a process called consolidation, during which the brain strengthens the neural connections that ____(2)____ a memory. Sleep appears to play a crucial role, for studies show that people who rest soon after learning ____(3)____ new material more accurately than those who stay awake. These findings suggest that memory is not a passive recording but an active reconstruction shaped by biology and behavior alike.

questions.q: Blank (1): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Long-term memory keeps information, so it 'retains' it for years. 'Erase' and 'release' mean losing it; 'distort' changes rather than stores it.

questions.q: Blank (2): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Neural connections that store a memory 'encode' it. 'Erase' and 'weaken' would destroy the memory; 'reveal' does not describe how connections hold information.

questions.q: Blank (3): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Remembering material more accurately means people 'recall' it better. 'Forget' and 'ignore' are opposites; 'invent' implies making it up rather than retrieving it.

title: The Origins of Agriculture
passage: Around ten thousand years ago, human societies in several parts of the world began to shift from hunting and gathering to farming. This transition, often called the Neolithic Revolution, did not happen overnight; rather, it ____(1)____ gradually as people learned to cultivate wild plants and domesticate animals. Farming allowed communities to produce a reliable food surplus, which in turn could ____(2)____ larger populations settled in one place. Yet the change brought new problems, including the spread of disease and greater social inequality. Archaeologists continue to ____(3)____ why so many independent cultures adopted agriculture within a relatively short span of time.

questions.q: Blank (1): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: A gradual transition 'unfolded' over time. 'Collapsed' and 'ceased' imply an ending, and 'reversed' means going backward, contradicting a forward shift.

questions.q: Blank (2): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: A food surplus can 'sustain' (support) larger populations. 'Displace' and 'scatter' would break up settlements; 'diminish' means to reduce, the opposite of supporting growth.

questions.q: Blank (3): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: An unresolved question is one scholars still 'debate.' 'Ignore' and 'dismiss' contradict ongoing interest; 'confirm' wrongly implies the matter is settled.

title: Catalysts and Enzymes
passage: Many chemical reactions proceed too slowly to be useful unless they are helped along by a substance called a catalyst. A catalyst works by providing an alternative pathway that requires less energy, thereby ____(1)____ the rate at which reactants are converted into products. Crucially, the catalyst itself is not consumed; it emerges from the reaction unchanged and can be used again. Enzymes, the catalysts found in living cells, are remarkably ____(2)____, each speeding up only one specific reaction among the thousands occurring at once. Without such molecules, the chemistry of life would be far too sluggish to ____(3)____ the needs of a living organism.

questions.q: Blank (1): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: A catalyst speeds a reaction, so it is 'accelerating' the rate. 'Reducing' and 'halting' would slow or stop it; 'disguising' does not describe changing a reaction rate.

questions.q: Blank (2): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Acting on only one specific reaction makes enzymes 'selective.' 'Unstable' and 'fragile' describe durability, not specificity; 'abundant' concerns quantity, not choosiness.

questions.q: Blank (3): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Reactions must be fast enough to 'meet' an organism's needs. 'Exceed' overstates the point, while 'delay' and 'ignore' contradict serving those needs.

title: Why Languages Change
passage: No language stays the same forever. Over many generations, the sounds, words, and grammar of a language gradually ____(1)____, so that speakers separated by centuries might scarcely understand one another. Contact with other languages accelerates the process, as words are borrowed and pronunciations shift. Some observers once regarded such changes as signs of decay, but modern linguists reject this view, arguing instead that variation is a ____(2)____ feature of every living language. What sounds like careless speech to one generation may become the accepted standard of the next. Linguists therefore ____(3)____ from labeling any dialect as inherently superior to another.

questions.q: Blank (1): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Languages that change over time 'evolve.' 'Freeze' means to stay fixed, the opposite; 'vanish' overstates loss, and 'repeat' does not convey gradual change.

questions.q: Blank (2): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Linguists see variation as a 'natural' feature of living languages. 'Harmful' echoes the rejected 'decay' view; 'temporary' and 'artificial' contradict its being intrinsic.

questions.q: Blank (3): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Rejecting the idea of superior dialects, linguists 'refrain' from labeling them (refrain from). 'Proceed' means the opposite; 'benefit' and 'emerge' do not fit the 'from' structure or sense.

title: The Carbon Cycle
passage: Carbon is constantly on the move, cycling among the atmosphere, the oceans, living things, and rocks. During photosynthesis, plants ____(1)____ carbon dioxide from the air, locking the carbon into their tissues. When plants and animals die and decay, or when they are burned, that carbon is ____(2)____ back into the atmosphere. For millions of years this exchange remained roughly in balance. The large-scale burning of fossil fuels, however, has ____(3)____ that balance, adding carbon to the air faster than natural processes can remove it and driving the shifts in climate that scientists observe today.

questions.q: Blank (1): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Plants take in carbon dioxide during photosynthesis, so they 'absorb' it. 'Emit,' 'release,' and 'expel' all mean giving it off, the reverse of what plants do here.

questions.q: Blank (2): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Decay and burning return carbon to the air, so it is 'released.' 'Absorbed' is the opposite; 'trapped' and 'buried' imply it stays locked away.

questions.q: Blank (3): choose the word that best completes the meaning.
questions.explanation: Fossil-fuel burning throws the balance off, so it 'disrupted' it. 'Preserved,' 'restored,' and 'reinforced' all suggest keeping the balance, contradicting the resulting climate change.

title: The Formation of Coral Reefs
passage: Coral reefs are among the most biologically diverse ecosystems on the planet, yet they arise from a remarkably simple partnership. The reef-building corals are tiny animals called polyps, which secrete a hard skeleton of calcium carbonate. Inside their tissues live microscopic algae that carry out photosynthesis, providing the polyps with much of their food. This intimate _____ benefits both organisms: the algae gain shelter and nutrients, while the corals receive energy. Because the algae require sunlight, reefs can only _____ in shallow, clear tropical waters where light penetrates easily. Over thousands of years, the accumulated skeletons of countless polyps build up into massive structures visible even from space. When ocean temperatures rise too high, however, the corals expel their algae in a process known as bleaching, which leaves them pale and vulnerable. Prolonged bleaching can be _____, killing colonies that took centuries to form. Scientists now monitor reefs closely, recognizing that these fragile systems serve as sensitive indicators of the broader health of the oceans.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 1.
questions.explanation: "Symbiosis" names a mutually beneficial partnership, which the passage describes; "rivalry" and "isolation" contradict cooperation, and "sequence" refers to order, not a relationship.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 2.
questions.explanation: "Flourish" means to thrive, fitting the reefs' growth in favorable waters; "dissolve" and "erode" imply breakdown, and "migrate" wrongly suggests reefs move.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 3.
questions.explanation: "Catastrophic" matches the killing of centuries-old colonies; "beneficial" is the opposite, "temporary" understates lethal damage, and "gradual" describes pace, not severity.

title: The Printing Revolution
passage: Before the mid-fifteenth century, books in Europe were copied by hand, a laborious task that made them scarce and enormously expensive. Only the wealthy and the clergy could afford them, and literacy remained the privilege of a small elite. Johannes Gutenberg's introduction of movable-type printing around 1450 fundamentally _____ this situation. By casting individual letters in metal that could be arranged, inked, and reused, his press could produce identical copies far more quickly than any scribe. The cost of books fell sharply, and their numbers multiplied. This sudden _____ of printed material accelerated the spread of new ideas across the continent. Scholars could compare texts, merchants could standardize records, and reformers could circulate their arguments to audiences no manuscript could ever reach. Some authorities, alarmed by the flood of unapproved writings, attempted to _____ the presses through censorship and licensing. Yet the technology proved impossible to contain. Within a few decades, printing shops had opened in cities throughout Europe, laying the groundwork for the Renaissance, the Reformation, and the scientific revolutions that followed.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 1.
questions.explanation: "Transformed" captures the sweeping change the press caused; "preserved" means to keep unchanged, "questioned" is too weak, and "postponed" implies mere delay.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 2.
questions.explanation: "Abundance" reflects the multiplied numbers of books; "shortage" and "decline" mean the reverse, and "concealment" contradicts wide circulation.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 3.
questions.explanation: "Suppress" fits alarmed authorities using censorship; "encourage" and "finance" suggest support, and "duplicate" describes copying, not control.

title: How Vaccines Train the Immune System
passage: The human immune system possesses a remarkable capacity to remember pathogens it has encountered before. When a virus first invades the body, specialized white blood cells slowly learn to recognize it and mount a defense, but this initial response is often too _____ to prevent illness. Vaccination exploits the immune system's memory without exposing a person to the dangers of the actual disease. A vaccine introduces a harmless fragment or weakened form of a pathogen, prompting the body to produce antibodies and memory cells as if it had faced the real threat. Should the genuine pathogen later appear, these primed defenses respond so rapidly that the infection is _____ before symptoms develop. The strategy has proven extraordinarily effective: smallpox, once a devastating killer, has been entirely _____ from the natural world through a global vaccination campaign. Diseases such as polio and measles have likewise been driven to the brink of elimination in many regions. Public health experts emphasize that widespread immunization protects not only vaccinated individuals but also those who cannot receive vaccines, by reducing the pathogen's ability to circulate.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 1.
questions.explanation: "Sluggish" explains why the slow first response fails to prevent illness; "aggressive" and "precise" would imply success, and "permanent" describes duration, not speed.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 2.
questions.explanation: "Neutralized" means the infection is stopped, matching primed defenses; "transmitted" and "magnified" worsen it, and "delayed" only postpones symptoms.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 3.
questions.explanation: "Eradicated" fits smallpox's complete removal; "isolated" implies mere containment, "recovered" and "inherited" do not describe eliminating a disease.

title: The Silk Road and Cultural Exchange
passage: The Silk Road was never a single paved highway but rather a sprawling network of overland routes linking China with Central Asia, the Middle East, and eventually Europe. For centuries, caravans of merchants carried goods across deserts and mountain passes, exchanging Chinese silk, Indian spices, and Persian textiles at bustling market towns. Yet the routes conveyed far more than luxury commodities. Along with merchandise traveled religions, technologies, and artistic styles, so that distant cultures gradually came to _____ one another's innovations. Buddhism spread from India into China, papermaking moved westward, and mathematical concepts passed between civilizations that never met directly. This constant _____ of ideas enriched every society the routes touched. Unfortunately, the same pathways that carried silk and philosophy could also transmit disease; historians believe the plague spread along these corridors with devastating effect. As maritime trade routes expanded in later centuries, the overland network gradually _____, and many of its once-thriving oasis cities faded into obscurity. Today archaeologists excavate their ruins, uncovering coins, manuscripts, and artifacts that testify to a remarkably interconnected ancient world.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 1.
questions.explanation: "Adopt" fits cultures taking up each other's innovations; "reject" and "outlaw" mean refusal, and "conceal" contradicts open exchange.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 2.
questions.explanation: "Circulation" describes ideas moving among societies; "suppression" and "shortage" contradict enrichment, and "duplication" misses the sense of flow.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 3.
questions.explanation: "Declined" matches the network fading as sea routes rose; "expanded" and "flourished" are opposites, and "diversified" does not explain the cities' obscurity.

title: Why Some Metals Resist Corrosion
passage: When iron is exposed to moist air, it reacts with oxygen to form rust, a flaky reddish compound that weakens the metal over time. This process, called corrosion, causes enormous economic damage, degrading everything from bridges to automobiles. Not all metals _____ so readily, however. Some, such as gold and platinum, are chemically inert and remain bright for centuries. Others owe their durability to a more subtle mechanism. Aluminum, for example, reacts instantly with oxygen, but the thin oxide layer it forms is dense and adherent rather than flaky. This film acts as a protective _____, sealing the surface and preventing further reaction with the metal beneath. Engineers exploit similar principles when they add chromium to steel to produce stainless steel, whose invisible oxide coating renders it highly _____ to rust. Understanding these reactions allows manufacturers to select the right material for each application, from surgical instruments that must never corrode to inexpensive structures where periodic repainting is acceptable. Corrosion science thus blends chemistry and engineering to extend the working life of the metal objects that surround us.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 1.
questions.explanation: "Corrode" continues the discussion of metals resisting rust; "harden," "conduct," and "expand" describe unrelated properties.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 2.
questions.explanation: "Barrier" fits a film that seals the surface; "catalyst" and "solvent" would promote reaction, and "conductor" relates to electricity, not protection.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 3.
questions.explanation: "Resistant" describes stainless steel not rusting; "prone," "sensitive," and "vulnerable" all imply susceptibility, the opposite meaning.

title: The Rise of Impressionism
passage: In the 1870s a group of French painters broke sharply with the polished conventions of the official art establishment. Rather than laboring for months in the studio to produce smooth, meticulously finished canvases, artists such as Claude Monet worked quickly outdoors, attempting to capture the fleeting effects of light and atmosphere. Their loose, visible brushstrokes and unblended colors struck many critics as crude and _____, and one reviewer coined the mocking label "Impressionism" after a Monet painting titled Impression, Sunrise. The name, intended as an insult, was soon embraced by the movement itself. What critics dismissed as carelessness was in fact a deliberate _____ to record perception as the eye actually experiences it, before the mind organizes a scene into tidy detail. Because the painters worked so rapidly, they could depict the same subject under changing conditions, showing how a haystack or a cathedral facade shifted in color from morning to dusk. Although initially _____, Impressionism gradually won acceptance and profoundly influenced the course of modern art, opening the door to still bolder experiments in the decades that followed.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 1.
questions.explanation: "Unfinished" matches critics seeing the loose brushwork as crude; "exquisite" and "priceless" are praise, and "traditional" contradicts the break with convention.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 2.
questions.explanation: "Attempt" fits a deliberate effort to record perception; "refusal" negates the goal, and "failure" and "accident" deny the described intent.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 3.
questions.explanation: "Ridiculed" fits the early hostility before acceptance; "celebrated" and "profitable" contradict it, and "forgotten" misses the active mockery described.

title: The Economics of Public Goods
passage: Economists distinguish between ordinary commodities and a special category known as public goods. A loaf of bread is a private good: if one person eats it, no one else can, and a baker can easily withhold it from anyone who refuses to pay. National defense, clean air, and street lighting behave quite differently. Once such goods are provided, it is nearly impossible to _____ anyone from enjoying them, and one person's use does not diminish what remains available to others. These two features create a well-known dilemma. Because individuals can benefit without paying, many are tempted to _____ contributing, hoping that others will bear the cost while they enjoy the result free of charge. If enough people reason this way, the good may never be supplied at all, even though everyone would gain from its existence. This is why economists argue that markets alone tend to _____ public goods, producing less than society actually wants. Governments therefore step in, funding such goods through taxation so that the benefits reach everyone regardless of individual willingness to pay.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 1.
questions.explanation: "Exclude" fits the impossibility of barring people from a public good; "protect" and "reward" miss the sense, and "distinguish" does not concern access.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 2.
questions.explanation: "Avoid" captures free-riders declining to contribute; "begin," "resume," and "double" all imply paying more, not less.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 3.
questions.explanation: "Underprovide" matches producing less than society wants; "overproduce" is the opposite, and "regulate" and "monopolize" describe other behaviors.

title: Plate Tectonics and Mountain Building
passage: The Earth's rigid outer shell is not a single continuous layer but is broken into roughly a dozen large plates that drift slowly over the hotter, more fluid material below. Though they move only a few centimeters each year, their interactions over millions of years have reshaped the surface of the planet. Where two continental plates collide, neither is dense enough to sink beneath the other, so their edges crumple and _____ upward, raising vast mountain ranges. The Himalayas, still growing today, formed in exactly this way as the Indian plate pushed relentlessly into Asia. Along other boundaries the plates instead _____ past one another, grinding sideways and periodically releasing built-up stress as earthquakes. Elsewhere plates pull apart, allowing molten rock to rise and create new crust along mid-ocean ridges. This grand cycle of creation and destruction is remarkably _____: crust generated at the ridges is eventually consumed where one plate dives beneath another, so the planet's surface is continually recycled. Recognizing these processes has allowed geologists to explain phenomena that once seemed unrelated, from the distribution of fossils to the location of volcanoes.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 1.
questions.explanation: "Buckle" describes crumpling edges rising into mountains; "flatten" and "scatter" contradict raising ranges, and "dissolve" is chemically wrong here.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 2.
questions.explanation: "Slide" fits plates grinding sideways past each other; "merge" and "descend" describe different boundaries, and "vanish" makes no sense for grinding plates.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 3.
questions.explanation: "Balanced" fits crust created and consumed in equal measure; "wasteful" and "random" misread the cycle, and "recent" contradicts millions of years.

title: The Psychology of Habit Formation
passage: Much of daily behavior is governed not by conscious decision but by habit, the automatic routines that free the mind to focus on other things. Psychologists describe a habit as a loop with three parts: a cue that triggers the behavior, the routine itself, and a reward that reinforces it. When a particular action reliably produces a satisfying outcome, the brain gradually _____ the connection between cue and response, so that the behavior eventually unfolds with little effort or awareness. This efficiency is enormously useful, allowing skilled drivers to navigate traffic or experienced typists to write without pondering each keystroke. The same mechanism, however, can entrench behaviors people would rather abandon. Because established habits are so _____, simply resolving to stop is rarely enough to break them. Researchers suggest that a more effective strategy is not to erase a habit but to _____ it, keeping the original cue and reward while substituting a healthier routine in between. A smoker who lights a cigarette to relieve stress, for instance, might learn to take a brief walk instead, satisfying the same underlying need through a different action.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 1.
questions.explanation: "Strengthens" fits reinforcement making behavior automatic; "weakens" and "delays" contradict the loop, and "ignores" makes no sense for the brain building a link.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 2.
questions.explanation: "Entrenched" explains why habits resist mere resolve; "fragile" and "recent" imply easy change, and "harmless" ignores the difficulty being discussed.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 3.
questions.explanation: "Modify" fits substituting a new routine while keeping cue and reward; "abandon" contradicts the strategy, and "reward" and "repeat" miss the change.

title: The Domestication of Plants
passage: The transition from hunting and gathering to farming ranks among the most consequential developments in human history. It did not happen overnight, nor was it the result of a single invention. Instead, early peoples in several regions of the world gradually _____ wild plants by saving and replanting the seeds of individuals with useful traits. Over many generations, this selective planting altered the plants themselves. Wild grasses, for example, tend to scatter their seeds as soon as they ripen, an adaptation that helps them spread but frustrates any harvester. Farmers unconsciously favored the rare plants whose seeds remained _____ to the stalk, and in time cultivated grains lost the ability to disperse on their own, becoming dependent on humans to sow them. Similar changes produced larger fruits, sweeter kernels, and reduced natural toxins. This slow reshaping of species is a striking example of how human choices can _____ the course of biological evolution. The resulting agricultural surplus supported denser populations, permanent settlements, and eventually the specialized crafts and institutions from which the first cities arose.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 1.
questions.explanation: "Domesticated" names the gradual taming through replanting; "harvested" and "discovered" are one-time acts, and "abandoned" contradicts cultivation.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 2.
questions.explanation: "Attached" fits seeds staying on the stalk for harvest; "loose" describes the scattering trait farmers avoided, and "hidden" and "poisonous" are irrelevant here.

questions.q: Choose the word that best completes gap 3.
questions.explanation: "Redirect" captures human choices steering evolution's course; "reverse" and "halt" overstate the effect, and "predict" describes foresight, not influence.
